name: Defunctionalization
summary: Reynolds 1972: replace higher-order functions with first-order datatypes — every lambda becomes a constructor of a sum type, and function application becomes a `match` over the sum. Whole-program transformation; eliminates closures entirely. Powers Roc lambda sets, MLton, some Idris backends, GPU compilers.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Reynolds's **defunctionalization** (1972, "Definitional Interpreters for Higher-Order Programming Languages") replaces higher-order functions with first-order datatypes: every lambda in the program becomes a constructor of a sum type, and function application becomes a `match` over the sum.

For a program with finitely many syntactic lambdas (which is any closed program), defunctionalization is a whole-program transformation that eliminates closures entirely, producing first-order code that is trivially amenable to C-style compilation. The sum type of "all possible function values" plus the global dispatch `apply` function replace the dynamic closure machinery.

sections.title: Mechanism
sections.content:
Collect every lambda in the program — including those produced by partial application or eta-expanded operators. Each lambda becomes a constructor of a global sum type `Closure`, with the constructor's fields being the captured variables. Every function call site that invokes a closure becomes a call to a generated dispatcher function `apply(closure, arg)` that pattern-matches on the constructor and runs the appropriate body.

The transformation is whole-program because the sum type must enumerate every lambda. A closed program has finitely many syntactic lambdas (the defunctionalisation theorem), so the sum is well-defined. Library code linked from outside breaks the assumption — the linker would need to know all lambdas in all clients to extend the sum.

After defunctionalisation, no first-class functions remain: the program is straightforwardly first-order and compiles via standard C-style codegen with no runtime closure allocation.

sections.title: Production deployments
sections.content:
Defunctionalisation is the theoretical backbone of:

- **Roc's lambda sets** — surgical defunctionalisation, where the compiler tracks the *exact* set of lambdas that can flow to each call site, then specialises the dispatcher per call site rather than maintaining a global sum type.
- **MLton** — whole-program SML compiler, defunctionalises plus monomorphises plus contifies as core passes.
- **Some Idris backends** — when targeting C or other separately-compilation-hostile environments.
- **GPU compilers** — closures cannot be allocated at runtime on most GPU programming models, so any higher-order user code must be defunctionalised before reaching the device.

The cost is losing separate compilation — defunctionalisation is fundamentally whole-program, so library-level code must either be generic over a closure type or pre-committed to the full set of callers.

sources.label: Danvy-Nielsen — Functional Correspondence Between Defunctionalization and CPS
